coredata.dc:title: Na+ functionalized carbon quantum dot incorporated thin-film nanocomposite membranes for selenium and arsenic removal
coredata.dc:creator: He, Yingran
Zhao, Die Ling
Chung, Tai-Shung
coredata.dc:description: Selenium (Se) and arsenic (As) contaminations in surface and ground water have become a perilous issue worldwide in recent years. In order to remove them proficiently and simultaneously, sodium ion modified carbon quantum dot (Na-CQD) incorporated thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes have been developed. Na-CQDs with an ultrafine size of 3.2 nm were synthesized from the pyrolysis of citric acid via a simple thermal treatment and then participated in the formation of polyamide (PA) layer during interfacial polymerization. The new Na-CQD modified TFN membranes exhibit enhanced pure water permeability (PWP) and rejection rates for toxic ions due to their higher surface hydrophilicity and reduced pore sizes compared to the original thin-film composites (TFC) membrane. Among them, the novel TFN membrane containing 0.05 wt% of Na-CQDs (TFN-0.05) displays the highest PWP of 10.4 LMH/bar and impressive rejections of 97.5%, 98.2% and 99.5% toward SeO3 2-, SeO4 2- and HAsO4 2-, respectively. In the simultaneous separation of Se and As, better ion rejection results are obtained. Meanwhile, the TFN-0.05 membrane validates superior antifouling propensity and robust long-term stability under the 180-h test. This study may deliver valuable insights in the preparation of nanoparticle modified polymeric membranes for water purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Thin-film nanocomposite (TFN)
Filtration membranes
Selenium and arsenic removal
Antifouling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Syntheses of sodium ion modified carbon quantum dots (Na-CQDs)
Fabrication of PES substrate, pristine TFC and Na-CQDs TFN membranes
Characterizations of the Na modified carbon quantum dots (Na-CQDs)
Characterizations of the TFC and Na-CQD modified TFN membranes
Effects of Na-CQD loading on NF performance
Antifouling properties and long-term stability of Na-CQD modified TFN membranes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank our group members for their kind help and suggestions. This research is supported by the Singapore National Research Foundation under its Environmental & Water Technologies Strategic Research Program, administered by PUB, Singapore's National Water Agency under the project " Advanced Development of Nanofiltration-Hollow Fiber membranes and their applications" ( 1301-IRIS-60 ) with NUS grant No. R-279-000-451-279 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis schemes of the sodium ion modified CQDs (Na-CQDs).
TEM images of as-prepared Na-CQDs (a)× 120 K and (b)× 200 K, (c) the corresponding size distribution and (d) the XPS wide scan spectrum of Na-CQDs.
Surface and cross-section morphology of PES substrate, pristine TFC and TFN membranes with Na-CQD loadings of 0.02, 0.05, 0.08 and 0.12 wt%.
XPS wide scan spectra of TFC and Na-CQD TFN membranes with different Na-CQD loadings.
ζ-potential as a function of pH for the membranes with different Na-CQD loadings.
Probability density function curves of the PES substrate, pristine TFC and Na-CQD modified TFN membranes.
Effects of Na-CQD loading on PWP and salt rejections of the TFC and TFN membranes.
Effects of Na-CQD loading on Se and As removal by the TFN membranes.
Effects of Na-CQD loading on the removal of mixed Se and As ions by the TFN membranes.

(a) Fouling tests for TFC and TFN membranes. Fluxes were plotted versus time for three periods: pure water for 12 h, 1 g/L BSA solution for 12 h, and pure water after hydraulic washing (2 h at a transmembrane pressure of 10 bar) for 12 h. (b) the corresponding total flux decline rates (FDR) and flux recovery ratios (FRR).
Evolution of pure water permeability and SeO32- rejection in 180 h using the TFN-0.05 membrane.
XPS analyses of the TFC and TFN membranes with different Na-CQD loadings.
Mean effective pore diameter (µp), geometric standard deviation (σp), molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) and contact angle (θ) of the membranes.
Benchmarking of NF membranes for selenium and arsenic removal.

head.ce:title: Na+ functionalized carbon quantum dot incorporated thin-film nanocomposite membranes for selenium and arsenic removal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NUS Graduate School for Integrative Science and Engineering, National University of Singapore, 28 Medical Drive, Singapore 117456, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: NUS Graduate School for Integrative Science and Engineering, National University of Singapore

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, National University of Singapore, 4 Engineering Drive 4, Singapore 117585, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, National University of Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Selenium (Se) and arsenic (As) contaminations in surface and ground water have become a perilous issue worldwide in recent years. In order to remove them proficiently and simultaneously, sodium ion modified carbon quantum dot (Na-CQD) incorporated thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes have been developed. Na-CQDs with an ultrafine size of 3.2 nm were synthesized from the pyrolysis of citric acid via a simple thermal treatment and then participated in the formation of polyamide (PA) layer during interfacial polymerization. The new Na-CQD modified TFN membranes exhibit enhanced pure water permeability (PWP) and rejection rates for toxic ions due to their higher surface hydrophilicity and reduced pore sizes compared to the original thin-film composites (TFC) membrane. Among them, the novel TFN membrane containing 0.05 wt% of Na-CQDs (TFN-0.05) displays the highest PWP of 10.4 LMH/bar and impressive rejections of 97.5%, 98.2% and 99.5% toward SeO3 2-, SeO4 2- and HAsO4 2-, respectively. In the simultaneous separation of Se and As, better ion rejection results are obtained. Meanwhile, the TFN-0.05 membrane validates superior antifouling propensity and robust long-term stability under the 180-h test. This study may deliver valuable insights in the preparation of nanoparticle modified polymeric membranes for water purification.

ce:section.ce:para: Selenium (Se) pollution in wastewater derived from industrial activities (i.e., farming irrigation, oil extraction and refining, metal casting, and coal mining) can accumulate in aquatic organisms and becomes a source of toxicity for many organisms including human being [1]. Both selenate (SeO4 2−) and selenite (SeO3 2−) are the main soluble inorganic species in contaminated surface water. On the other hand, arsenic (As) is regarded as one of the most fatal elements and its predominant oxidation states in water are As (III) and As (V). These toxic As ions can cause severe health issues such as skin discoloration, lesions, and cancers [2].

Se and As removal from wastewaters can be challenging and pricy due to the combination of high volume but low concentrated (low ppm or ppb) polluted streams [3,4]. There are several treatment methods including chemical precipitation, adsorption, ion exchange, membrane filtration, chemical reduction and biological removal [5–10]. Nanofiltration (NF) has received intensive attention recently for water treatment owing to its capability and sustainability [11–19]. Azamat et al. designed MoS2 nanosheet membranes with appropriate hourglass shape pores in the surface to remove As ions and the simulated results showed a high membrane permeability and rejection [20]. Besides, Aguiar et al. investigated a hybrid system including NF and RO membranes to purify Se and As ions in the mine drainage. In their hybrid system, commercialized NF99 and RO membranes (HR98PP) were utilized to reject those ions [21]. Elcik and his co-workers employed commercial membranes (NF90 and NF270) to separate arsenic with a rejection of 90% [22]. Gao et al. fabricated hollow fiber membranes functionalized with chelating polymers to remove arsenic with a pure water permeability (PWP) of 2 LMH/bar and high rejection [23]. He and her coworkers synthesized a hydrophilic zwitterionic co-polymer, P[MPC-co-AEMA], and developed novel thin-film composite (TFC) membranes to remove Se and As with a PWP of 8.5 LMH/bar and a rejection higher than 98% [24]. Nevertheless, the major challenge currently is how to design a membrane which possesses both high PWP and rejection at the same time.

As an emerging class of carbonaceous nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with diameters less than 10 nm, have drawn tremendous attention due to their low toxicity, excellent water dispensability as well as ease of syntheses and functionalization [25–27]. By tuning the precursors and reaction parameters, the resultant CQDs can have various functional groups on the surfaces, such as amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl and other oxygenous groups which make CQDs highly water soluble and biocompatible [28–31]. Benefiting from these strengths, CQDs have been applied to enhance membrane performance of pure water permeability and antifouling property. Zhao and her co-workers reported a CQD modified pressure-retarded osmosis (PRO) membrane which possessed a higher power density and enhanced antifouling propensity [32]. Gai et al. synthesized three kinds of CQDs (i.e., O-CQD, Na-CQD-5 and Na-CQD-9) and embedded them into the polyamide (PA) layers of PRO membranes. Among them, the thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes containing Na-CQD-9 exhibited the highest water flux of 53.54 LMH and power density of 34.20 W/m2 [33].

In order to further explore the applications of Na+ modified CQDs (Na-CQDs) in membrane technologies, in this work for the first time we studied the influence of Na-CQD incorporation on NF membranes to separate selenium and arsenate ions. The purposes of this work are to explore the influence of Na-CQDs chemistry and loading on (1) membrane formation, (2) separation performance to simultaneously purify Se and As ions, (3) antifouling properties and (4) the long-term performance stability.

Citric acid (99.5%) and sodium hydroxide (98%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich for the synthesis and functionalization of CQDs. The polyethersulfone (PES) polymer, N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone, polyethylene glycol 400 were purchased from Solvay, Merck and Sigma Aldrich, respectively, for the fabrication of membrane substrates. Piperazine (PIP, Sigma-Aldrich), 1, 3, 5-benzenetricarbonyltrichloride (TMC, Sigma-Aldrich), and hexane (Fisher Chemicals) were used to synthesize the TFC layer on top of PES substrates. Several chemicals including ethylene glycol, diethylene glycol, glucose, sucrose and α-cyclodextrin hydrate were received from Alfa Aesar for pore characterizations and various salts such as NaCl, MgCl2, MgSO4, Na2SO4, Na2SeO3, Na2SeO4 and Na2HAsO4 were ordered from Sigma-Aldrich for flux and rejection tests. Bovine serum albumin (BSA, Sigma-Aldrich) was employed as the feed solution in antifouling tests. All the chemicals are analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Syntheses of sodium ion modified carbon quantum dots (Na-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The Na-CQD synthesis process was based on the previous method [31] with further functionalization as illustrated in Fig. 1. In a typical reaction, solid citric acid powders were placed into a glass beaker covered with a glass slide and then heated in an oven at 180 °C for 3 h to form CQDs passivated with carboxyl groups. Then, a 5 M NaOH solution was added to adjust the CQD aqueous solution to pH 9. During the alkalization process, the carboxylic acid groups on the surface of CQDs reacted with hydroxyl groups to form alkalized Na-CQDs. Subsequently, the product solution was dialyzed in a commercial dialysis membrane until the conductivity of surrounding DI water was steady. After that, the Na-CQD powders were freeze dried from the aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of PES substrate, pristine TFC and Na-CQDs TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Fabrication of the PES substrates and pristine TFC membranes could be found in our previous paper [34]. The Na-CQD TFN membranes were made by adding Na-CQDs into the PIP solutions with Na-CQD loading varying from 0, 0.02, 0.05, 0.08–0.12 wt%. The resultant TFN membranes were denoted accordingly as TFN-0.02, TFN-0.05, TFN-0.08 and TFN-0.12.

The synthesized Na-CQDs were characterized by a high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100F). The corresponding histograms of particle size distribution were estimated by counting 150 nanoparticles. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JEOL JSM-6700) was used to investigate the membrane morphology. The surface chemistry of the membranes was examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD spectrometer, Kratos Analytical Ltd.) with a monochromatized Al Kα X-ray source (1486.71 eV photons), while their surface charge was measured by a SurPASS electrokinetic analyzer (Anton Paar GmbH) in the pH range of 2–11.

Neutral rejection probes of 200 ppm were used to quantify the molecular weight cut off (MWCO), effective mean pore size and pore size distribution of the membranes [35–38]. A total organic carbon analyzer (TOC ASI-5000A, Shimazu, Japan) was utilized to detect the concentrations of organic solutes. When the rejection was 90%, the MWCO was calculated. The following equation could be employed to determine the membrane's probability density function curve [35]:
(1)

2

where
d
p
is the pore diameter. The mean pore size (μ
p ) is the pore diameter when R= 50% and the geometric standard deviation (σ
p ) is the ratio of
d
p
when R= 84.13% and 50%.

The static contact angle was tested by a contact angle goniometer (Rame Hart, USA). The samples were freeze-dried before the tests. The average value from ten random locations was used to minimize the experimental errors.

The membrane performance was tested under the pressure of 10 bar at room temperature. Pure water permeability (PWP, Lm−2 h−1bar−1, abbreviated as LMH/bar) was tested by using DI water, while rejection (R, %) measurements were carried out using feeds with different salt concentrations. PWP and R were calculated by the following equations:
(2)
PWP
=
Q
A
∆
P
(3)
R
( % )
=
(
1 −
C
p
C
f
)
×
100 %
where Q (L/h) is the water flux at the permeate side, ΔP (bars) is the testing pressure, and A (m2) is the operative membrane area. A conductivity meter (Metrohm AG) and inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, Optima 7300DV, PerkinElmer) were employed to measure Cp and Cf, which are the solute concentrations in the permeate and feed sides. The concentration was 1000 ppm for each ion solute, while the molar ratio of 3 ions, SeO3 2-, SeO4 2- and HAsO4 2- in the mixed solutions was 1:1:1.

In the fouling resistance tests, BSA was used as the representative protein and organic foulant. The testing solution had 1 g/L BSA and the operational pressure was 10 bar. The steady flux of DI water after running for 12 h was noted as Jw1. Afterwards, the feed was changed to a BSA solution, JBSA was recorded after running for another 12 h. Then the NF membranes were backwashed by DI water for 1 h with a transmembrane pressure of 10 bar. Finally, Jw2, the steady water flux of the cleaned membrane was measured again. The following equations were used to calculate the total flux decline rate (FDR) and flux recovery ratio (FRR):
(4)

=
(
1 −
J
BSA

w
1
)
× 100 %

FRR
=
(
J
w
2
J
w
1
)
× 100 %

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the Na modified carbon quantum dots (Na-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2(a) and (b), TEM images of Na-CQDs demonstrate well dispersed and spherical dot-like nanoparticles. They exhibit a narrow size distribution between 2.5 and 4.0 nm with an average diameter of 3.2 nm (Fig. 2(c)). The XPS image (Fig. 2(d)) shows the elemental composition of Na-CQDs. The mass ratios of Na, O, N and C are 14.50%, 40.33%, 0.22% and 44.95%, respectively. The ultrafine size as well as the presence of sodium and oxygen-containing groups lead to the outstanding hydrophilicity of Na-CQDs, which disperse well in the solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the TFC and Na-CQD modified TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of Na-CQDs on the morphologies of the PES substrate, TFC and modified TFN membranes was characterized by FESEM. As shown in Fig. 3, the TFC membrane has a surface layer full of globules and nodules due to the interfacial polymerization between PIP and TMC. By contrast, the Na-CQD modified TFN membrane exhibits more intense grainy and convex structures with a rougher surface. This is because Na-CQDs may play two roles when binding with PIP or TMC in the reaction: On the one hand, when the loading is low, the hydrophilic Na-CQDs enlarge the interface between the aqueous and organic solutions because their hydrophilic functional groups give rise to the repulsion to the organic phase [39]. On the other hand, the solubility and diffusivity of PIP molecules are enhanced by Na-CQDs through hydrogen bonding, which accelerates the kinetics of selective layer formation and changes the microstructure of the selective layer accordingly [40]. The thickness of the TFC layer is around 200 nm. With the increase of Na-CQD loading, the selective layer becomes thicker and it is generally consistent with the trend of PWP (shown later). In other words, CQDs can be readily integrated into the state-of-the-art TFC membranes (e.g. RO, NF and PRO membranes) by taking advantages of their small sizes and rich surface functional groups that can form chemical bonds with other compounds.

Fig. 4 shows the XPS results of the TFC and Na-CQD modified TFN membranes. The peaks at 284.6, 532.0, 400.5 and 1071.4 eV represent C, O, N and Na elements, respectively. As expected, Na is barely detected because of its low loading in the PA layers of all TFN membranes. Table 1 tabulates the evolution of weight percentage of these elements as a function of Na-CQD loading. The N/O ratio of the TFN membranes drops as the Na-CQD loading increases, which implies the decrease of cross-linking degree of the PA active layers [41]. Remarkably, the O/C ratios of the TFN membranes are higher than that of the pristine TFC membrane. This could be the reason of the oxygen-containing functional groups from Na-CQDs and the changes of cross-linking degree of the PA active layer, providing the evidence of the successful incorporation of Na-CQDs [42].

The zeta potential data of TFC and TFN membranes are displayed in Fig. 5. All membranes are negatively charged mainly owing to the carboxyl deprotonation [43]. It is notable that the surface of the TFN membranes becomes more negative when the Na-CQD content in the selective layer increases. This can be the reason of the plentiful carboxyl groups on the surface of Na-CQDs. Therefore, negative ions in the feed solutions can be efficiently rejected by these membranes due to electrostatic repulsion.

Fig. 6 shows the pore size distribution curves of the PES substrate, TFC and TFN membranes, while Table 2 presents their pore properties and contact angles. The PES substrate has the largest average pore diameter of 13.64 nm, following by the TFC membrane which has an average pore diameter of 0.94 nm due to the interfacial polymerization. It further drops to 0.51 nm when the Na-CQD loading is 0.12 wt% because the nanoparticles fill in the pores of the selective layer. Furthermore, their corresponding distribution curves become slimmer and sharper which contributes to the higher rejection of TFN membranes.

As displayed in Table 2, the contact angle of the PES substrate is 57.2° because it was cast from a formula containing a large amount of PEG [44]. Therefore, its contact angle is lower than those normal PES membranes. The contact angle decreases with the deposit of a PA selective layer on its top surface and further monotonously decreases with the incorporation of Na-CQDs in the selective layer. Therefore, the contact angle is 34.9° for the TFN-0.02 membrane but drops to 23.5° for TFN-0.12. This phenomenon could be rationalized as the inclusion of Na-CQDs influences the surface microstructure as well as the hydrophilicity provided by the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, thus affects the wettability of membrane surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of Na-CQD loading on NF performance
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 summarizes the effects of Na-CQD loading on separation performance of the resultant membranes. The TFC membrane has a relatively low PWP value of 5.4 LMH/bar because of the dense PA layer. The incorporation of Na-CQDs leads to the improved hydrophilicity of PA layers, which is beneficial for water transport. The PWP value of TFN-0.05 dramatically increases to 10.4 LMH/bar which is about 2 times of the TFC membrane. However, a further addition of Na-CQDs in the PA layer results in a decrease in water flux. This could arise from the effect of a thicker PA layer because the addition of hydrophilic Na-CQDs improves the PIP diffusivity and accelerates its transfer to the interface. Moreover, the larger nanoparticle loading could also cause the pore blocking on the PA layer.

The salt rejections consistently rise as the Na-CQD loading increases because of smaller pore size and pore size distribution (Fig. 6). The rejections of salts with SO4 2- are higher than those of salts with Cl-. Since the separation mechanisms of NF membranes include the molecule sieving and electrostatic repulsive theory, the membranes with negative charges have stronger repulsive forces against SO4 2- (divalent anion) than Cl- (monovalent anion) [14]. Moreover, the hydrated radius of SO4 2- ions is larger than that of Cl- ions [45]. Therefore, the membranes have a higher rejection to SO4 2- than to Cl-.

Fig. 8 exhibits the membrane separation to Se and As ions as a function of Na-CQD loading when single ion solutions are used as the feeds. The best result is achieved at a 0.12 wt% loading of Na-CQDs (TFN-0.12). Compared to the TFC membrane, the rejection to SeO3 2- jumps from 82.4% to 98.6% and the rejection to HAsO4 2- rises from 91.3% to 99.5%. These enhancements arise principally from the reason that Na-CQDs results in the selective layer with a small pore size and narrow pore size distribution as shown in Fig. 6. However, the TFN-0.12 membrane does not have the highest PWP.

Regarding to the exclusion of Se ions, the modified TFN membranes exhibit higher rejections to Se (VI) than to Se (IV) since the Se (VI) solution is dominated by divalent SeO4 2- anions, while the Se (IV) solution is a combination of HSeO3 - and SeO3 2- anions. Additionally, the modified TFN membranes have the best performance to HAsO4 2- among all three ions, possibly owing to two reasons: (1) The hydrated radius of HAsO4 2- is larger than that of SeO4 2 [46] and (2) the membrane surface exhibits different electronegativity at different solution pH. The modified TFN membranes have larger electrostatic repulsions to HAsO4 2- anions because its solution has a pH value of 8.6, which is greater than the SeO4 2- feed solution of 8.0.

The practicability of using Na-CQD modified TFN membranes for simultaneous removal of Se and As is evaluated in Fig. 9. The rejections of Se in the mixed solution are slightly higher than those in single ion solutions, a similar trend has been observed in Fig. 8. This phenomenon arises from the tiny pH variance among these feed solutions. In other words, the mixed ion feed has a pH value of about 8.5 while the single SeO3 2-, SeO4 2- and HAsO4 2- feeds have pH values of 7.5, 8.0 and 8.6, respectively. In contrast, the rejection of As is similar for both single and mixed ion solutions because of the close pH values.

To benchmark this study with other existing membranes, TFN-0.05 is selected as the candidate in Table 3. It can be noticed that the membranes available in the markets have relatively low PWP values and Se and As rejections, while the most lab-made ones exhibit better PWP and higher ion rejections. By comparison, the newly fabricated TFN-0.05 membrane has comparably high PWP and salt rejections among these membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling properties and long-term stability of Na-CQD modified TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Membrane fouling is an inevitable phenomenon in NF applications which greatly disfavors water permeation, weakens NF performance, and ultimately shortens the lifetime of membranes [47,48]. Hence, enhancing the antifouling property of NF membranes is vital [49]. In this study, fouling resistance of Na-CQD modified TFN membranes were assessed by using BSA as a model foulant. Fig. 10 (a) illustrates the testing protocol where three types of water flux are measured. After reaching a steady state using DI water as the feed for 12 h, a BSA solution of 1 g/L is fed for another 12 h. Immediately, fluxes of all membranes drop as a result of the BSA deposition on membrane surfaces. After the filtration of BSA, the membrane surfaces were backwashed with DI water at a transmembrane pressure of 10 bar for 1 h. All water fluxes were recovered to some degrees. Fig. 10 (b) compares the corresponding total flux decline rates (FDR) and flux recovery ratios (FRR).

Generally, a membrane with a lower FDR and a higher FRR has better antifouling properties. Therefore, the results from Fig. 10 confirm that (1) the fouling resistance of PES membranes against proteins is largely enhanced by the addition of Na-CQDs, and (2) the greater the Na-CQDs loading in the selective layer, the better the antifouling properties. The TFN-0.12 membrane shows the best anti-fouling performance because (1) its FDR is only 25.2%, which is roughly one-half of that (50.5%) for the pristine TFC membrane, and (2) its FRR is 93.4% compared to 63.5% of the pristine TFC membrane. Clearly, the presence of Na-CQDs not only facilitates water transport by creating more channels within the PA layer, but also promotes the formation of a hydration layer on the membrane surface against foulants [50–52].

In order to confirm the capability and stability of the Na-CQD modified TFN membranes, a continuous 180-h test was conducted. Unlike many TFN membranes which suffer from leaching of nanoparticles during long-term operations due to poor compatibility between nanoparticles and selective layer [53,54], the TFN-0.05 membrane displays steady NF performance with the final PWP of 10.2 LMH/bar and rejection of 99.9%, as shown in Fig. 11. The stable performance may be realized by the good interactions between hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of Na-CQDs and the PA selective layer.

In summary, novel TFN membranes have been designed by incorporating Na-CQDs during the interfacial polymerization. Due to their ultra-fine size and rich functionality, Na-CQDs can be easily dispersed in the PA selective layer to efficiently manipulate the surface structure and hydrophilicity of the TFN membranes. TFN-0.05 exhibits the superior performance among all the membranes, with an impressive water flux of 10.4 LMH/bar and rejections of 97.5%, 98.2% and 99.5% against SeO3 2-, SeO4 2- and HAsO4 2-, respectively. The enhanced performances are in accordance with the smaller surface pores and more hydrophilic PA selective layers. The impressive NF efficiency, together with the robust antifouling properties, environmental friendliness and easy synthesis process make this work promising for the development of next generation Na-CQD based membranes in the field of water treatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank our group members for their kind help and suggestions. This research is supported by the Singapore National Research Foundation under its Environmental & Water Technologies Strategic Research Program, administered by PUB, Singapore's National Water Agency under the project "Advanced Development of Nanofiltration-Hollow Fiber membranes and their applications" (1301-IRIS-60) with NUS grant No. R-279-000-451-279.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no completing financial interest.
